message:
fix(fp-types): preserve dtype in nanmin/nanmax instead of collapsing to Float64

`nanmin` and `nanmax` in fp-types were both implemented via `collect_finite`
followed by `f64::min`/`f64::max` over a `Vec<f64>`, which silently promoted
every reduction to `Scalar::Float64(_)`. This was wrong for any dtype-preserving
aggregation: an Int64 column would round-trip through `nanmin` and come back
as Float64, a Utf8 column would return `Scalar::Null(NaN)` instead of the
lexicographic minimum, and Bool columns were not handled at all.

Rewritten both functions to walk the slice once, threading the current
best `Scalar` through and comparing like-with-like:

  * Int64 vs Int64: integer `<` / `>`
  * Float64 vs Float64: IEEE `<` / `>` (matches legacy behavior)
  * Utf8 vs Utf8: lexicographic
  * Bool vs Bool: false < true
  * Mixed numeric types: fall back to `to_f64()` on both sides so that a
    mixed Int64/Float64 column still produces a well-defined result without
    forcibly collapsing integer inputs.

`is_missing()` is used instead of `collect_finite` so that Utf8/Bool values
are no longer discarded as "non-finite", while NaN/Null entries are still
skipped.

When the input contains no non-missing values, both functions still return
`Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN)`, matching the previous empty-input contract.

fp-groupby: update the two GroupBy min/max unit tests that baked in the old
"everything becomes Float64" behavior — they now assert `Scalar::Int64(10)`,
`Scalar::Int64(20)`, `Scalar::Int64(30)`, `Scalar::Int64(40)` for integer
inputs, which is what pandas itself returns.

No public API changes; this is a correctness fix to the nanmin/nanmax
contract and the tests that over-specified the wrong behavior.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
